conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me first review the eligibility rules, and then I'll ask you some questions.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to ask you a few questions about Alex to determine your eligibility.

**How old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 21 years old, I need to ask:

**Is Alex in any approved education or training?**

(Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they're in approved education or training, but typically stops at age 20.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. He finished his studies a while back.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a few more questions to ensure I have all the details I need.

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Bristol.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I just need to confirm a couple more details:

**Do you currently live with Alex, or does he live elsewhere?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex doesn't live with me anymore. He moved out and lives on his own now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. One final question:

**Do you contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep (such as paying for food, clothes, accommodation, or other expenses) at least the same amount as Child Benefit would be?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I do help him out a bit when I can - I give him some money towards food and bits and pieces, but it's not a huge amount. Maybe around £20 a week or so.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the information you've given me and the official Child Benefit rules, I need to inform you of my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Unfortunately, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why: Child Benefit is normally available for children under 16. It can continue for young people aged 16-19 (but under 20) if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex is 21 years old and is not in approved education or training, he is beyond the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility.

Even though you're contributing towards Alex's upkeep, the financial contributions cannot extend eligibility beyond the age limits set out in the rules.

If you have any other questions or if Alex's circumstances change (though at 21 and not in education, the age limit has already passed), please feel free to ask.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 21 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 21, which is 20 or over; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child is 21, which is 20 or over
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old and is not in approved education or training. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16. Child Benefit can continue if the child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training, but Alex is 21 and not in education or training. Therefore, the age eligibility criteria are not met. Even though you contribute approximately £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this does not extend eligibility beyond the age limits specified in the rules.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 21 years old and not in approved education or training. Alex does not live with the claimant. Child Benefit eligibility ends when a child turns 20 (if in approved education/training) or at 16 if not in education/training. Since Alex is 21 and not in education or training, he exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility, regardless of any financial contributions made by the claimant.

Even though you contribute approximately £20 per week towards Alex's upkeep, this does not extend eligibility beyond the age limits specified in the rules.

Child Benefit eligibility ends when a child turns 20 (if in approved education/training) or at 16 if not in education/training. Since Alex is 21 and not in education or training, he exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility, regardless of any financial contributions made by the claimant.
